title: Reading Financial News
objectives: Read and interpret a NZX company announcement
Identify what financial news is material versus noise
Explain how information releases affect share prices

vocabulary.definition: Information that a reasonable investor would consider important in deciding whether to buy, hold or sell a share. Listed companies must immediately disclose material information to the NZX.
The legal obligation of NZX-listed companies to immediately release any material information to the market. Prevents insiders from trading on information the public does not have.
A company's forecast of its own future earnings, issued to help investors set expectations. Often more market-moving than the actual earnings result.
When a company signals its earnings will be below previous guidance or market expectations. Almost always causes an immediate share price fall.
Illegal trading on material non-public information — for example, buying shares before a positive announcement that is not yet public. Heavily penalised under NZ financial markets law.

activities.title: Continuous Disclosure — Why Information Must Be Public
activities.notePrompt: Write: What is continuous disclosure? Why is it important for markets to work fairly? What happens to a company that fails to disclose material information on time?
activities.content:
The NZX operates on a principle of continuous disclosure — listed companies must immediately release any material information to the market. This levels the playing field between professional investors and ordinary shareholders.

What must be disclosed immediately:
— Profit warnings or upgrades
— Major contracts won or lost (if material to earnings)
— CEO or CFO resignation or appointment
— Proposed mergers, acquisitions or divestments
— Significant legal proceedings
— Dividend announcements
— Capital raisings

Why it matters: Without continuous disclosure, company insiders and their friends could trade on non-public information before the market knows. This is insider trading — illegal in NZ and carries criminal penalties including up to 5 years imprisonment and fines up to $1 million under the Financial Markets Conduct Act 2013.

When companies fail to disclose: The FMA (Financial Markets Authority) investigates. Companies can face significant fines. Directors can face personal liability. The NZX can also suspend trading in the company's shares until adequate disclosure is made.

Practical effect: when a major announcement is released through the NZX, algorithmic trading systems process the information in milliseconds. Share prices can move substantially within seconds of an announcement — before you have time to read it properly.

activities.title: Types of NZX Announcements and Their Impact
activities.notePrompt: For each announcement type listed, estimate whether it typically causes a share price rise, fall or minimal movement, and explain why.
activities.content:
NZX announcements vary enormously in market impact. Here is a guide.

High impact — typically large price moves:
— Profit warning: 'Earnings will be below previous guidance' → sharp fall, often 10%+
— Earnings upgrade: 'Earnings will exceed previous guidance' → sharp rise
— Takeover bid: Someone is offering to buy the company at a premium → share rises toward offer price
— CEO resignation (unexpected): Uncertainty → fall
— Major contract loss: Significant revenue impact → fall

Medium impact:
— Half-year or full-year results in line with expectations: Modest movement depending on guidance
— New contract win (material but not transformational): Modest rise
— Dividend announcement in line with expectations: Small positive response

Low or no impact:
— Share buyback programmes (already announced): Already priced in
— Routine AGM results where no contested votes: Minimal
— Administrative filings (director share transactions, registry changes): Usually trivial

The key principle: markets move on surprises, not events. An earnings result exactly in line with expectations may cause no price movement at all — it was already priced in. The same earnings result announced with an unexpected downgrade in guidance could cause a 15% fall even if the result itself was good.

activities.statement: If a NZ company's CEO has early knowledge of an upcoming profit upgrade, they can legally buy the company's shares immediately as this information will soon be public.
activities.explanation: False. Trading on material non-public information is insider trading — illegal under the Financial Markets Conduct Act 2013 regardless of when the information will become public. It does not matter that the information will be disclosed soon. The CEO is prohibited from buying shares until the information is publicly disclosed through the NZX. NZ insider trading penalties include up to 5 years in prison and fines up to $1 million for individuals.

activities.title: How to Read a Company Announcement
activities.notePrompt: Read the fictional Mainfreight announcement below. Write: What is the key information? What impact would you expect on the share price? What would you do if you held Mainfreight shares?
activities.content:
Example NZX Announcement — Mainfreight Limited:

Date: [date]
Subject: FY2025 Full Year Earnings Update

'Mainfreight advises that for the full year ended 31 March 2025, the Group expects to report net profit after tax of approximately $360 million to $370 million, compared to analyst consensus of $345 million. This outcome reflects stronger-than-expected performance across the Americas and Asia divisions.

Group revenue is expected to be approximately $5.1 billion, ahead of prior year. The Board expects to declare a full year dividend of $1.60 per share, up from $1.50 in the prior year.

A formal earnings release will be made on [date].'

Analysis:
— This is a positive earnings surprise: $360-370M vs $345M expected = 4% to 7% above expectations
— Revenue also ahead of prior year
— Dividend increased from $1.50 to $1.60 (+6.7%)
— Effect: expect a meaningful share price rise on release — likely 3% to 8% given the magnitude of the beat
— If holding Mainfreight: this is positive confirmation of investment thesis — no reason to sell

The phrase 'analyst consensus' is key — market prices already reflect expected results. The announcement beats that consensus, so prices will rise.

questions.question: Fisher and Paykel Healthcare releases full-year results showing net profit of $280 million. The market had expected $320 million. What is the most likely immediate market reaction?
options: Share price rises — $280 million profit is still a large, positive number
Share price falls significantly — the result is $40 million below what the market had expected and priced in
No movement — listed companies' results are already factored into prices before they are released
Share price rises slightly — any positive profit result causes a small positive market reaction
questions.explanation: Markets move on surprises versus expectations, not absolute results. $280 million profit sounds impressive, but if the market expected $320 million, the $40 million shortfall is a negative surprise — and the share price will fall accordingly. This is called 'missing analyst estimates' and often triggers sharp price falls even when results are historically strong. The market prices in its best estimate of results in advance; the announcement simply corrects that estimate.

questions.question: A NZX listed company CFO learns the company's annual earnings will be 30% above consensus. Before the announcement, she buys 50,000 shares in the company. This is:
options: Acceptable — the information will be public soon so no real advantage is taken
Discouraged but not illegal — the FMA will request she sell the shares back
Illegal insider trading under the Financial Markets Conduct Act 2013 — criminal penalties include up to 5 years imprisonment
Only illegal if the share purchase is above a certain dollar threshold
questions.explanation: This is a textbook case of insider trading — trading on material non-public information. The CFO has information (30% earnings beat) that is not yet public and would significantly affect the share price. Trading on this information is illegal under the Financial Markets Conduct Act 2013 regardless of the amount involved or the intention to disclose the information soon. Penalties include up to 5 years imprisonment and fines up to $1 million.

activities.title: High, Medium or Low Impact? Sort These Announcements

pairs.item: Company issues profit warning — earnings will be 25% below guidance
CEO resigns unexpectedly with no successor named
Half-year results exactly in line with analyst consensus
Company wins material contract with a new international customer
Takeover bid received at 35% premium to current share price
Routine change to company share register

activities.explanation: High impact announcements contain major surprises relative to expectations — earnings misses, unexpected leadership changes, takeover bids, or contract losses. Medium impact announcements are meaningful but expected or partially anticipated. Low impact announcements contain no new material information or confirm what was already expected.

activities.statement: When a company announces earnings exactly in line with analyst expectations, the share price typically rises because the company has confirmed positive performance.
activities.explanation: False. Markets are forward-looking and prices reflect expectations in advance. When a company delivers results exactly in line with what the market already expected, there is no new information — so there is no reason for the price to move significantly. 'In line' results typically produce minimal price movement. It is surprises — positive or negative — that move share prices. This is why guidance and analyst forecasts matter so much: they define the baseline against which actual results are judged.

activities.question: What is the legal obligation for NZX-listed companies to immediately release any information that would materially affect their share price?
activities.hint: Two words — it describes an ongoing, real-time obligation to release information.
activities.explanation: Continuous disclosure is the fundamental transparency requirement of public markets. Without it, insiders could exploit information advantages at the expense of ordinary investors. NZX Rule 3.1 requires listed companies to release material information 'immediately' — typically within minutes of becoming aware of it. The FMA and NZX police compliance and companies can face significant penalties for failure to disclose.

activities.title: Find a Real NZX Announcement
activities.prompt: Go to nzx.com/markets/nzsx/announcements and find one announcement made in the past week. Identify: the company, what the announcement says, whether it is high/medium/low impact, and what happened to the share price after it was released. Write four sentences summarising what you found.
activities.nceaLink: Extra Learning — reading real NZX announcements is the fastest way to build practical investment knowledge.
